concept_ref: §3 row 3 + §1 'sub-condition can never change value across all tests — masked by the others'

scenario.title: Admin Override With Negation
scenario.narrative: A panel is shown when the user is NOT an admin or has an explicit override. The QA engineer claims Branch Condition coverage is met with three tests. Read carefully — the negation is the trap.

claim: Which is the STRONGEST coverage level the test set below actually satisfies?
charges: The defendant function show_panel(is_admin, has_override) shows a panel when the user is NOT an admin OR has an explicit override.
The QA team submits a 3-test suite — (admin=F, override=F), (admin=F, override=T), (admin=T, override=F) — and claims the suite satisfies MC/DC.

The prosecution alleges that under short-circuit evaluation of `(!is_admin) || has_override`, the override condition is masked when is_admin is FALSE, breaking the MC/DC independence requirement for has_override.

options.label: Statement only
options.explanation: Too weak. Statement is satisfied (every line runs), but so are Decision and BC — pick the strongest level that is actually met.

options.label: Decision (and therefore Branch + Statement)
options.explanation: Decision IS satisfied — but so is Branch Condition. is_admin is observed T (T3) and F (T1/T2); has_override is observed T (T2) and F (T1/T3). BC is also fully met, so Decision is not the STRONGEST level.

options.label: Branch Condition (each condition T and F)
options.explanation: Correct. is_admin observed T (T3) and F (T1/T2); has_override observed T (T2) and F (T1/T3) — BC ✓. MCDC fails for has_override: T1↔T2 flips only has_override but outcome stays T (because !is_admin=T already covers the OR). No valid override-only-flip pair exists in T1..T3.

options.label: MCDC — independence pairs for both conditions
options.explanation: TRAP. MCDC for is_admin succeeds (T1↔T3 flips only is_admin, outcome flips T→F). MCDC for has_override fails: T1↔T2 flips only has_override but the outcome stays T because !is_admin=T already satisfies the OR. With short-circuit on (!is_admin) when is_admin=F, has_override is masked. To reach MCDC you would need (T,F)+(T,T) — neither (T,T) is in the set.

correct_answer_explanation: BC is the strongest level that holds. MCDC for is_admin is reachable via T1↔T3, but MCDC for has_override is masked: T1↔T2 only flips has_override yet the outcome stays TRUE because !is_admin=T already saturates the OR. You'd need (T,T) in the test set to surface a valid override independence pair — and it isn't there.
wrong_answer_explanation: Read each condition at the SOURCE level: is_admin is the literal variable, not `not is_admin`. Branch Condition coverage requires is_admin and has_override each to be observed as T and F.
hints: Read each condition at the SOURCE level: is_admin is the literal variable, not `not is_admin`. Branch Condition coverage requires is_admin and has_override each to be observed as T and F.

Tally for is_admin and has_override independently. is_admin: F in T1/T2, T in T3 ✓. has_override: F in T1/T3, T in T2 ✓. Both conditions observed T and F — BC is satisfied. Now ask: is MCDC also satisfied?

Decision: T1/T2 give true, T3 gives false → ✓. BC: each of is_admin and has_override observed T and F → ✓. MCDC for is_admin: T1↔T3 flips only is_admin and outcome flips → ✓. MCDC for has_override: T1↔T2 flips only has_override but outcome stays T (!is_admin=T saturates the OR). No valid override-pair exists. Strongest satisfied = BRANCH CONDITION.

seeded_faults.description: The override flag is read from the wrong variable; the bug is masked when is_admin is FALSE because short-circuit evaluation skips the second operand.

misconceptions.explanation_md: Negations and short-circuit operators trick learners into thinking that if every condition appears as T and F at the source level, then everything stronger is automatically satisfied too. BC is satisfied here, but has_override can never independently flip the outcome when is_admin=False — exactly the kind of masking effect MCDC is designed to surface.
